Questions.Title: Matplotlib's original design inspiration
Questions.QuestionText: On which plotting interface was Matplotlib originally modeled?
Questions.Hint: Think about a well-known numerical computing environment used in engineering.
Questions.Feedback: Matplotlib was originally modeled after MATLAB's plotting interface, giving developers fine-grained control over every aspect of a figure.

Questions.Title: Seaborn's relationship to Matplotlib
Questions.QuestionText: Which statement best describes the relationship between Seaborn and Matplotlib?
Questions.Hint: Consider whether Seaborn works independently or depends on another library for rendering.
Questions.Feedback: Seaborn is not a replacement for Matplotlib but a complement to it, extending its capabilities while relying on Matplotlib for rendering.

Options.Text: Seaborn complements Matplotlib and relies on it for rendering
Seaborn is a complete replacement for Matplotlib
Seaborn renders figures independently without Matplotlib
Seaborn is a lower-level library that Matplotlib is built on

Questions.Title: Seaborn's pandas DataFrame integration
Questions.QuestionText: Why is Seaborn especially popular in data science workflows?
Questions.Hint: Think about what data structure data scientists commonly use in Python.
Questions.Feedback: Seaborn integrates directly with pandas DataFrames and automatically handles many formatting details, making it popular in data science workflows.

Options.Text: It integrates directly with pandas DataFrames and handles formatting automatically
It produces 3D visualizations that Matplotlib cannot create
It is faster than Matplotlib for all types of charts
It does not require Python to be installed

Questions.Title: Recommended environment for visualization setup
Questions.QuestionText: What type of environment is recommended when setting up Python for advanced visualizations to avoid dependency conflicts?
Questions.Hint: Think about isolated Python environments commonly used in data science.
Questions.Feedback: Using a virtual environment or conda environment is recommended to avoid dependency conflicts, especially when working with Jupyter Notebooks.

Options.Text: A virtual environment or conda environment
A global Python installation shared across all projects
A Docker container running a non-Python base image
A cloud-only environment with no local packages

Questions.Title: Matplotlib's core organizational structure
Questions.QuestionText: In Matplotlib, what object contains one or more Axes objects, each representing an individual plot area?
Questions.Hint: Think about the top-level container in Matplotlib's hierarchy.
Questions.Feedback: Matplotlib organizes plots around a Figure object that contains one or more Axes objects, each representing an individual plot area within the figure.

Questions.Title: Best chart type for categorical comparison
Questions.QuestionText: Which chart type is described as the go-to visualization for comparing discrete categories or groups?
Questions.Hint: Think about the chart whose bars clearly show differences in magnitude between groups.
Questions.Feedback: Bar charts are the go-to visualization for comparing discrete categories or groups, making differences in magnitude immediately apparent.

Questions.Title: Seaborn function for bar charts
Questions.QuestionText: Which Seaborn function is used to create bar charts?
Questions.Hint: Seaborn function names follow the pattern sns.[charttype]plot().
Questions.Feedback: In Seaborn, sns.barplot() is used to create bar charts for categorical comparisons.

Questions.Title: Confidence intervals in Seaborn line plots
Questions.QuestionText: Which Seaborn function can automatically add confidence intervals when data has repeated measurements?
Questions.Hint: Think about the Seaborn equivalent of Matplotlib's plt.plot().
Questions.Feedback: sns.lineplot() can automatically add confidence intervals when data contains repeated measurements, which is one of its key advantages over Matplotlib's plt.plot().

Questions.Title: Encoding additional dimensions in scatter plots
Questions.QuestionText: In Seaborn's sns.scatterplot(), which parameters can be used to encode additional data dimensions beyond the two numeric axes?
Questions.Hint: Think about visual channels other than position that can represent data values.
Questions.Feedback: sns.scatterplot() supports hue, size, and style parameters to encode additional dimensions, effectively turning a 2D plot into a 4D or 5D visualization.

Questions.Title: Histograms and bin width selection
Questions.QuestionText: What is the consequence of selecting too few bins in a histogram?
Questions.Hint: Think about what happens to the level of detail shown in a distribution.
Questions.Feedback: Too few bins obscure detail in a histogram, while too many bins introduce noise. Bin width selection is critical for accurate distribution representation.

Options.Text: Detail in the distribution is obscured
The histogram automatically switches to a KDE curve
Individual data points become visible on the chart

Questions.Title: Heatmap color encoding purpose
Questions.QuestionText: In a Seaborn heatmap, how are numerical values represented?
Questions.Hint: Think about the visual channel that varies across the grid cells.
Questions.Feedback: Heatmaps encode numerical values as colors, mapping values to a color gradient automatically, making them ideal for displaying correlation matrices.

Options.Text: As colors mapped to a color gradient
As bar heights within each grid cell
As dot sizes proportional to the values
As text labels only, with no color encoding

Questions.Title: Limitation of correlation heatmaps
Questions.QuestionText: What is a key analytical limitation of correlation heatmaps?
Questions.Hint: Consider what type of mathematical relationships a Pearson correlation measures.
Questions.Feedback: Correlation heatmaps reveal only linear relationships, so non-linear dependencies may be missed without supplementary analysis. Correlation also does not imply causation.

Options.Text: They reveal only linear relationships and miss non-linear dependencies
They can only display positive correlations, not negative ones
They require data to be normally distributed before use
They cannot display more than ten variables at once

Questions.Title: Recommended pair plot configuration
Questions.QuestionText: Which combination of parameters is described as a common professional configuration for Seaborn pair plots?
Questions.Hint: Think about settings that simultaneously reveal trends, distributions, and group separability.
Questions.Feedback: Combining kind='reg' with diag_kind='kde' is a common professional configuration that simultaneously reveals trends, distributions, and group separability.

Questions.Title: Seaborn's set_theme() context options
Questions.QuestionText: Which context options does Seaborn's set_theme() provide to scale elements for different output formats?
Questions.Hint: Think about the different venues where a figure might be presented.
Questions.Feedback: Seaborn's set_theme() offers contexts like 'paper', 'talk', and 'poster' to scale elements appropriately for different output formats.

Questions.Title: Colorblind-friendly palette recommendation
Questions.QuestionText: Which color palettes are recommended for accessibility in publication-quality figures?
Questions.Hint: Think about palettes specifically designed for viewers with color vision deficiencies.
Questions.Feedback: For accessibility, colorblind-friendly palettes like 'colorblind' or 'cividis' should be preferred when creating publication-quality figures.

Questions.Title: Matplotlib's global font configuration mechanism
Questions.QuestionText: Which Matplotlib mechanism is used for global font configuration across all figures?
Questions.Hint: Think about a dictionary-like object in Matplotlib that stores default settings.
Questions.Feedback: Matplotlib's rcParams dictionary is the central mechanism for global font configuration, while individual text elements can be overridden with keyword arguments.

Questions.Title: Preferred format for print-quality export
Questions.QuestionText: Which file formats are preferred for print publications because they preserve quality at any scale?
Questions.Hint: Think about formats that describe graphics mathematically rather than as pixels.
Questions.Feedback: Vector formats like SVG and PDF preserve quality at any scale and are preferred for print, while PNG at 300 DPI is a common raster standard for journal submissions.

Questions.Title: Truncated y-axis misleading visualization example
Questions.QuestionText: According to the module, what is a classic example of a misleading visualization design choice?
Questions.Hint: Think about what happens when the y-axis does not start at zero.
Questions.Feedback: A classic example of a misleading visualization is a y-axis that starts at 95 instead of 0, making a 2% difference appear enormous and distorting the story the data tells.

Options.Text: A y-axis starting at 95 instead of 0, making a small difference appear enormous
Using a bar chart instead of a pie chart for categorical data
Adding a confidence interval to a line plot
Using a colorblind-friendly palette in a presentation

Questions.Title: Plotly's high-level API comparison
Questions.QuestionText: Which Plotly component provides a high-level API that mirrors Seaborn's simplicity while producing interactive outputs?
Questions.Hint: Think about the module within Plotly designed for quick, expressive chart creation.
Questions.Feedback: Plotly Express provides a high-level API that mirrors Seaborn's simplicity while producing rich interactive outputs compatible with Jupyter and web dashboards.

Questions.Title: Activating Matplotlib interactive mode in Jupyter
Questions.QuestionText: Which magic command activates interactive mode for pan, zoom, and figure inspection in Jupyter notebooks using Matplotlib?
Questions.Hint: Think about the magic command that enables widget-based interactivity in Jupyter.
Questions.Feedback: Using %matplotlib widget or %matplotlib notebook magic commands in Jupyter activates interactive mode for pan, zoom, and figure inspection.
